title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"

title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"
body:
The thing i noticed is
That this professor is teaching at such a prestigious university but still is grounded
He does not take pride in his intelligence, he didnt leave any chance of appreciating other's responses
That's what makes him great
People who are actually Intelligent does not make the other person feel dumb
They only know how to improve themselves and others...

title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"

title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"
body: Imagine watching this on your phone in a distant village and your granny asks what are you watching and you cannot even explain her about Harvard because she doesn't know anything about academics and education due to generational deprivation. But now her Grandchild is having direct access to Harvard on their phone. Such a great thing Science did for Social justice and equality.
author: @an_artie

title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"

title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"
body: I took philosophy although not in Harvard.  In over half a century living on this planet I have never found an experience that was more painful and rewarding than beginning to learn how to think more thoroughly.  The world would be vastly different if we all had exposure to philosophical thinking.
author: @dinnerwithfranklin

title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"
body: "Once the familiar turns strange, it's never quite the same again." ..."Self-knowledge is like lost innocence. However unsettling. It can never be unthought or unknown"

title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"

author: @HaroonKhan-gp4vj

title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"
body: About 7 years ago when I lived down the street from Harvard, Professor Sandel was still teaching this course and the public could sit in on all the lectures -- just walk into Sanders Theater and take a seat.  It was especially fascinating because you got to hear so many different questions from the students, and thus learned so much even after watching all the lectures here on YouTube. At the end of class you could get in line to meet him - he always took the time to chat with anyone who wanted to meet him.
author: @evviemoore5166

title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"
body: This man is an example of a real teacher for life. He is so passionate and takes his job seriously, you can tell he means what he says and cares to make his message clear. I've learned so much just from this one hour.
author: @NoNeedJustFeed

title: Justice: What's The Right Thing To Do? Episode 01 "THE MORAL SIDE OF MURDER"
body:
Jeez people, calm down. These are quite clearly first year students, they're still probs 18 - 21. These discussions massively enrich their thinking capabilities, for many of them this is the first time they've ever contemplated such moral dilemmas. THIS is how universities create the thinkers of our societies. All you people are just envious of their opportunities - get over it.

People that go to Harvard are just that - people.

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body: My purpose is to empower as many students as possible so I put together a mass document of ALL 13 of my FREE guides PLUS 5 NEW college admission courses so just DM me “MENTOR” to my Instagram @UltimateIvyLeagueGuide and I’ll send it to you ✅😊
author: @ultimateivyleagueguide

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body: I’ve seen studying until the ice melts, studying until the candle dies out, but I’ve never seen studying until the paper runs out!! 😰🙈
author: @sophie_59306

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body:
I am an 18-year-old girl living in South Korea, and although I should be a high school student, I dropped out of school two years ago. I wasn't interested in studying (to be precise, I hated schoolwork—after dropping out, I taught myself Spanish and French, and now I can speak them fluently). Yes, I just didn't want to do it. For the past two years, I’ve been working part-time jobs to make money. I thought I’d probably just keep living like this until I die. That was before I watched your video three weeks ago.

I’m sure many people have told you this before, but: my life has changed. After watching your video, I started thinking about what my life would have been like if I had gone to college. I’ve wanted to be an astronomer for a long time. I thought about it for a week, and eventually quit my part-time job. I returned to my parents’ house. (I had been living alone because I dropped out of school and had a strained relationship with my parents.) My parents were surprised but listened quietly to my story. I reconciled with them and am now preparing to return to high school. I want to tell you that it’s all thanks to you. I’m truly grateful...😊🎉
author: @YanYanhome

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body: You did a full gym workout, ate healthy meals and did skincare with all that studying, girl I am impressed. Most people are like gym who? Skin what? Ramen and Red Bull baybee.
author: @sunnyandthechlo

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body: I think the most unrealistic part of this is the fact that you had the motivation to actually get ready and look put together 💀 you best know I would look like a Shrek and Lord Farquaad hybrid on finals week
author: @cursedtortilla101

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body: "studying until you RAN OUT OF PAPER" I have a lot of respect for anyone who wakes up and does something challenging like that successfully.
author: @NextGenGames0

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body: "Sleep deprived" is exactly how I would describe my university experience. I remember hating every second of my last year (I loved my practices but HATED everything related to my classes and thesis), but nowadays I look back at that time with so much admiration. I learned so much.
author: @myBquest

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body: Another finals week hack: record yourself speaking your notes then playback when on the treadmill. I would hit the gym twice a day because of all the sitting/studying. You're still studying, but also exercising and reducing stress. 🤞🏾
author: @Sonia-rn1zz

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body: I have to say, one of my biggest struggles when studying is trying to stay productive but then getting distracting, or even finding that my teacher has given me bonus work for midterms, and I don't donut, but everyone else does. So honestly, props to you.
author: @talia6965

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body:
This is why people like her go to Harvard. She woke up, recorded herself, made herself launch and dinner, ran to the store, went to the library to study, and then had time to make 2 video calls?! I lock myself in my room and only eat and take a shower.😂
Though, to be fair, and despite the humor, I appreciate this video, clearly pressuring myself is not doing me any favor. I tried this semester to manage my time better, and I will continue to do so until hopefully I can be like her!
author: @LeoBenH

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog

title: *BRUTALLY REALISTIC* Harvard Finals Week Vlog
body: Sometimes I feel like I threw away a lot of opportunities when I applied for an average uni with the grades I had. But watching these videos reassures me that those famous unis are not the place for me. I’d probably give up and if I fought through it I’d feel like it was all for nothing. All I want is to have a comfortable life and I know that this uni will give me that, so why push myself to the breaking point yk? At the same time tho I always wonder how far I could have gone had I wanted to… Life decisions…

title: The Lowest GPA Someone Got Into Harvard With
body:
Either:
1. It's one of those gifted kids who really sucked at school because they couldn't be bothered.
2. It's someone with parents who bought them their way in.
3. It's someone who was predominantly focused with other spheres of their life (Sports, Handling Poverty, Business, Charity Work) and got recognized for it.
4. It's a combination of one of the above to varying lesser degrees.
author: @hydra5758

title: The Lowest GPA Someone Got Into Harvard With
body: I understand that there have been scandals where people were buying themselves into prestigious colleges, but in nearly all those cases the students weren’t academically poor just average. What’s interesting about this is that for whatever reason they were admitted, it wasn’t because of money. While very rare it’s not unorthodox to see a few outliers with bad GPAs get into Ivy Leagues. This is because the student most likely showed tremendous accomplishments outside of class (ex: sports, entrepreneurship, leadership, etc.)
author: @toastycarp

title: The Lowest GPA Someone Got Into Harvard With
body: Y’all forget people who work also go to Harvard. They might not achieved much in their old schools, but they might’ve made significant impacts in their line of work.
author: @marurinaf.6119

title: The Lowest GPA Someone Got Into Harvard With
body: What a lot of people don't get is colleges also care about what high-school you went to.  I had a 2.5 GPA, was not in any clubs or sports, and was not top anything, yet I was accepted at a 3.5 minimum GPA school and I had a 920 on my sat and I didn't didn't show them my sat.  Covid helped considering it made many colleges no longer ask for an sat but yeah, I learned that apparently my high-school was really rich and had a lot of renown so I was accepted over 1000 4.0 and above GPA kids. It's stupid and I don't deserve it honestly
author: @tortellinifettuccine

title: The Lowest GPA Someone Got Into Harvard With
body: Key word "self-reported." While it is likely that there is an outlier with a 2.9 GPA this cannot be known for certain.

title: You Need to Be Bored. Here's Why.
body: I’ve noticed that even just watching a show isn’t enough stimulation for me, I also have to scroll on my phone, play a game, etc. it’s like I need double stimulation. It’s horrible. I want to get rid of my smartphone
author: @Pem_321

title: You Need to Be Bored. Here's Why.
body: This explains why all my best creative ideas come to mind while I take a shower, because that is the only period of time when I take my eyes off the screen.
author: @andyhuang5987

title: You Need to Be Bored. Here's Why.
body:
I don’t use Facebook, Snapchat, or Instagram, and neither does my younger brother. At just 18 years old, he has already achieved things that many people struggle to achieve even by the age of 50.

So thank you, sir, but I am not part of this social media race. I only use YouTube for music while I’m driving, working, or simply listening.

I believe peace, focus, and real success matter more than chasing attention online.
author: @UnsungSpartan

title: You Need to Be Bored. Here's Why.
body: I used to fill every silence with noise, every pause with distraction. Thought I was being productive, staying connected. But there's something terrifying about sitting with nothing, isn't there? Just you and the questions you've been avoiding. I remember reading something that made me put my phone down mid-scroll: "In the space between thoughts, you'll find what you've been searching for in all the wrong places." It was from What the Silence Tried to Say by Alira Sennel. Made me realize I'd been running from the very emptiness that might actually hold my answers. Now I try to sit with the boredom, let it wash over me. And you know what? Those uncomfortable questions it brings up - they're not trying to hurt me. They're trying to show me who I really am underneath all the noise.
author: @SaenRiven

title: Harvard CS50 – Full Computer Science University Course
body:
(00:00:00) Lecture 0 - Scratch

(00:00:00) Introduction to Scratch
(00:10:32) Basic Scratch commands and blocks
(00:25:45) Using variables in Scratch
(00:45:12) Conditional statements and loops in Scratch
(01:15:20) Creating simple games in Scratch
(01:45:08) Lecture 1 - C

(01:45:08) Introduction to C programming language
(02:10:22) Syntax and basic data types in C
(02:35:57) Control structures in C
(02:55:43) Arrays and strings in C
(04:13:23) Lecture 2 - Arrays

(04:13:23) Introduction to arrays
(04:25:10) One-dimensional arrays and operations
(04:43:58) Multidimensional arrays and matrix operations
(06:20:43) Lecture 3 - Algorithms

(06:20:43) Introduction to algorithms
(06:30:15) Searching algorithms (linear and binary search)
(06:45:02) Sorting algorithms (selection, insertion, bubble, merge, quicksort)
(08:37:55) Lecture 4 - Memory

(08:37:55) Introduction to computer memory
(08:48:20) Memory hierarchy and cache
(09:02:10) Pointers and dynamic memory allocation
(11:03:17) Lecture 5 - Data Structures

(11:03:17) Introduction to data structures
(11:15:40) Stacks and queues
(11:30:05) Linked lists
(11:47:55) Trees and binary search trees
(13:15:36) Lecture 6 - Python

(13:15:36) Introduction to Python programming language
(13:28:10) Python syntax and basic data types
(13:40:48) Control structures and loops in Python
(13:58:21) File handling and modules in Python
(15:39:25) Lecture 7 - SQL

(15:39:25) Introduction to SQL and relational databases
(15:52:10) Basic SQL queries (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)
(16:05:45) Joins and subqueries in SQL
(18:00:55) Lecture 8 - HTML, CSS, JavaScript

(18:00:55) Introduction to web development technologies
(18:12:40) HTML structure and tags
(18:25:15) CSS styling and selectors
(18:38:50) JavaScript basics and DOM manipulation
(20:23:38) Lecture 9 - Flask

(20:23:38) Introduction to Flask web framework
(20:35:55) Routing and rendering templates with Flask
(20:48:20) Handling forms and user input in Flask
(22:39:01) Lecture 10 - Emoji

(22:39:01) Introduction to emoji encoding and representation
(22:50:15) Using emojis in text-based applications
(24:02:50) Cybersecurity

(24:02:50) Introduction to cybersecurity concepts and threats
(24:15:30) Network security and encryption techniques

body: Currently in university in England and a few years in now; I have never seen a lecture at this quality and nor have I ever seen a lecturer so enthusiastic, who loves his job more, loves to share knowledge more but most importantly I’ve never seen a lecturer who explains concepts in a way that is easy for people who have never seen the content in their life. Usually, 98% of lecturers talk as if you already have your masters. His methods of teaching and showing in person analogies is outstanding. The fact this is free is unbelievable. Excellent job, Sir.

title: Day in the Life: Heart Surgeon
body: As a paramedic, what he says at 2:35 is so true. Its really only "scary" when you have tried what you know should work/ MUST work, but it dosnt. An intubation for example, for your first time is definitely nerve wracking for obvious reasons, but after the first time its not scary until you nailed the whole process only to find its still not working, then you really have to think haha.

title: Day in the Life: Heart Surgeon
body: Fun fact: this man saved my fathers life. I will never forget the hours, minutes, seconds I sat waiting with my mom outside of surgery doors. For him to calmly come out, put his hand on my shoulder, and told me everything was going to be alright. Truly the nicest, most humble doctor I have ever met. If you ever read this sir, thank you. - Mark

title: What Makes a Good Life? Lessons from the Longest Study on Happiness | Robert Waldinger | TED
body: I’m 61, married for 33 years, one daughter 25 years old. Went to college and worked my whole life. Planning to retire at the end of this year. Been lucky, worked hard, lived a good life, very few regrets....hope to be around for many more years. I am no expert but I can offer my thoughts for what they’re worth: pick your relationships based on decency and character, not flash; be prepared to work hard on improving yourself (relationships and education and physical self); live within your means; invest time and money on your health, family first; things don’t last but memories do; and always be thankful. I think as you get older, friends become more important.  Good luck to all in finding your own happiness.  👍🏼👍🏼
author: @Mikey-wg2xu

title: What Makes a Good Life? Lessons from the Longest Study on Happiness | Robert Waldinger | TED
body:
Old man here. Stunningly good talk. And 100% true. I spent so much of my time when I was younger working hard and worrying about our finances. And I am glad we worked hard and planed for the future.

But I am more glad my wife and I also took time for each other and for our two sons. We did everything together. Sports, vacations, weekend outings, reading books out loud, praying together. Now my joy is spending time with my lovely wife, and our weekly calls with our grown sons who are still close to us. I wish everyone could experience the joy of being loved and understood.
author: @andrewheffel3565

title: What Makes a Good Life? Lessons from the Longest Study on Happiness | Robert Waldinger | TED

body: My beloved grandfather died at 98, he has been a widower since he was 42. He never remarried, but he lived a life of hard work, always trying to be of help for his friends. He died remembering his lost wife, but never complaining about bad things of life. I still remember his teachings and his strength like they are etched in stone.

body:
6:12 Good relationships keep us happier and healthier.

3 lessons:
6:27 Social connections are good, loneliness kills.
7:20 Quality of your close relationships matters.
9:05 Good relationships protect our bodies and brains.
author: @jwh0122

title: What Makes a Good Life? Lessons from the Longest Study on Happiness | Robert Waldinger | TED
body:
Here in 2025. The message is so well delivered that everything he said makes total sense. I am entering my 30's and most people i speak to talk about the memories in childhood and how things were better back in the day.

The root cause of happiness was people. Having friends, family and togetherness is the ultimate form of happiness. I moved out in my 20's and came back home almost 10 years later to realize not everything can be perfect but if you are closer to family, the hope to make a better life will never die. To those , if your family isn't perfect - you would always struggle to make the connections.. I know a few people that find it hard but in reality, its the experiences that make up the real you. Learn to love, trust and engage with people as much as you can. I wish I knew this earlier but glad life has its own ways of showing things to you.

He also mentioned something that defines to "forgive".. people who hold grudges are often the most miserable people as the subconscious mind is never freed up for positive experiences. Love the message.
author: @nofearwriting8892

title: What Makes a Good Life? Lessons from the Longest Study on Happiness | Robert Waldinger | TED

title: I lost everything at 9. A decade later, I’m at Harvard running a 7-figure business…here’s my journey
body:
Lately, I’ve been sitting with the weight of my own privilege.

It’s strange…I’ve worked so hard to get to where I am, and I’m proud of everything I’ve built.

But there’s this guilt.
Like… who am I to have all this?
To be recognized, celebrated, and given opportunities that my parents could only dream of?

They sacrificed more than I’ll ever fully understand.
My community supported me in ways I’m only now seeing.
And part of me wonders if it’s okay to enjoy what I’ve have when so many others haven’t had the same chance.

But I’ve been trying to reframe that guilt. Instead of letting it eat away at me, I want it to keep me grounded. I want it to remind me of why I do what I do.

If I have privilege, I want to wield it responsibly and make it easier for the next student to succeed. If I’ve reached a place of influence, I want to honor those who got me here.

So with this privilege, I choose to reach back, extend a hand, and make sure someone else gets to climb, too.
author: @ultimateivyleagueguide

title: I lost everything at 9. A decade later, I’m at Harvard running a 7-figure business…here’s my journey
body: I just need people who see this not to feel bad if the most you can do after similar trauma is just survive. Make no mistake: this is trauma, and it WILL come to collect the cost eventually if you do not take time to rest and heal. Many trauma survivors overachieve through their 20s and then crash hard because your body simply cannot suffer so much and then do so much without stopping forever. Other survivors don’t or can’t ever reach this level of normative success and that is OKAY. Success looks different for all of us; a simple, peaceful life is the goal for many. Rest before your body chooses a time to force rest for you.

title: Asking Harvard Students If They Ever Sleep
body: Excellent video. We are all striving for financial freedom and a better way of life. It is not difficult to achieve this with wise investing, economical lifestyle, and careful budgeting. I'm pleased I learnt early on to work hard for financial independence.
author: @Rubyeverly-Jessica

title: Asking Harvard Students If They Ever Sleep
body: If you're lucky enough to have supportive parents then you can dedicate upward of 60 hours a week to your studies and still sleep 7-8 hours, but if you do have to work full-time that only leaves you 20 to 30 hours and you're already sleep deprived at this point.
author: @damienroy5846

title: Asking Harvard Students If They Ever Sleep
body: i'd really like to go to Harvard myself, and it is reassuring to know that it isn't just hours hours of work where you can't catch a single break, makes me slightly confident in the thought that i could do good there. my road towards Harvard is still long though, and that is if it happens.
author: @Darthmessiah66

title: How To Get Into Harvard (from India)
body: i think she forgot to mention that her father Ajit Nagral is a millionaire and founder of multiple companies. In fact her father and mother were major donors to Harvard Divinity School. (a new program made possible by a generous gift from Vibhu and Ajit Nagral out of their interest in strengthening the Hindu presence on campus.) The Price of Admission book by Pulitzer prize winning journalist Daniel Golden details multiple such cases in his book of Ivy league admissions.
author: @ckeds

title: How To Get Into Harvard (from India)
body: This video actually shows how you should look at education system, not as a burden, but as an opportunity to grow yourself in the field you are interested about. Really helpful video. Thank you.

title: How To Get Into Harvard (from India)
body:
Don’t waste your time. I’ll tell you how to get into Harvard.

1. Great school. Like (The Cathedral and John Connon School) which had tatas ambanis as alumns

2. Great connections

3. Money

4. Support system (parents, recommenders, network),…

5. Desire, passion, and braveness to go away from home to learn. Or it can also be that your family has a support
System in USA to take care of you.
author: @sachinkumars9883

title: Harvard’s RICHEST Students #harvard #college #harvarduniversity #university
body: My cousin went to another IVY league and worked in the library on campus. A student came up to the front desk begging to let him borrow a book because it was crucial for his report. She denied his request and afterwards her coworker laughed at her telling her she had just told the Prince of a country in the Middle East that he couldn't borrow a book.

body: I had my Harvard Business School reunion last month.  No joke -- on the reunion registration form, they asked if we would be traveling with a security detail, and if so, to please describe the detail.

title: Harvard’s RICHEST Students #harvard #college #harvarduniversity #university
body: Even though I didn’t go to Harvard university, but I also have a story about rich students. I graduated from Beijing Language and Culture university (i was a foreign student there), and we had a student from Kuwait who bought Lamborghini in Beijing, which is double price in China and drove this fancy car to campus every day. And he rented an apartment just across the campus , and the rent was about 2 thousands $ which is pretty expensive for Beijing

author: @sofiansulaiman4308

title: Harvard’s RICHEST Students #harvard #college #harvarduniversity #university
body: I know someone who has a 100% scholarship to a boarding school in Canada. Obviously, they're international boarding schools, so there are people from all over the world. Anyway, one day he told me he was very surprised because one of his friends was complaining because he would have to spend spring break at his grandfather's Italian estate and not at the cabin near a ski slope in Switzerland... It sounds crazy, but it's his reality. Or the time another of his classmates were talking about how boring it was to own castles in the UK. These kids generally have families with generations of money, the famous "Old Money," and many of their ancestors had castles and now they occupy them as a form of investment, but they live so locked up in their bubble that their problems are a privilege for others
author: @Nattrg

title: Harvard’s RICHEST Students #harvard #college #harvarduniversity #university
body: I didn't go to Harvard but I went to a top-tier rich kids school as a middle-class person on nerit scholarships and it was humiliating how poor I felt. I had to work as a freaking janitor while some of my classmates had private jets to go all over the world. They had off-campus huge apartments and suites. They have their car on campus. One girl's dad owned an airline.
author: @captaincrunch784

title: Harvard’s RICHEST Students #harvard #college #harvarduniversity #university
body: I went to an Ivy Leage equivalent UK university that was known for having Oxford and Cambridge rejects (yes, including me), and I would hear these stories quite often.. You would even see people walking in town with plaid and dripping in wealth. I was a regular student living in the cheapest student accommodation on government scholarship from a working class ethnic family who worked a part-time to pay my daily needs. Many of my housemates were like this, including one of my friends who in one semester only had 5 pounds per week for food. Seeing rich people spend their money away like it was water and not caring about their studies like we did used to make us so mad.
author: @lenanana8
